article_title: EPFL and CSEM Break 30% Efficiency Barrier With Perovskite-Silicon Triple-Junction Solar Cell

content_preview.title: EPFL and CSEM Break 30% Efficiency Barrier With Perovskite-Silicon Triple-Junction Solar Cell
content_preview.summary: Swiss researchers set a new certified record of 30.02% efficiency for a triple-junction solar cell combining silicon with two perovskite layers, published in Nature.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at EPFL's Photovoltaics and Thin-Film Electronics Laboratory and CSEM, the Swiss Center for Electronics and Microtechnology, have achieved an independently certified efficiency of 30.02% for a perovskite-silicon triple-junction solar cell — the highest ever recorded for this class of device. The work, published in [*Nature*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10385-y) on March 17, 2026, surpasses the previous certified record of 27.1% and marks a significant step...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 587-word News article on a genuine materials science breakthrough — the first certified >30% efficiency for a perovskite-silicon triple-junction solar cell. Good use of bullet-point format for technical details, appropriate 'What We Don't Know' section on manufacturing scalability and durability.
editor_notes.source_verification: 4 sources reviewed. (1) EPFL news release — 200 OK; confirms 30.02% certified efficiency, 54 cm² device, previous 27.1% record, 1.4V open-circuit voltage for top cell (article says 1.405V — acceptable rounding from primary paper), 2018 at 13% efficiency, Christophe Ballif 'well above 40%' quote, Kerem Artuk as lead author, publication in Nature. Does not use chemical name '4-hydroxybenzylamine' — uses general description. (2) Nature paper — paywalled, not directly readable; appropriately cited as primary source. (3) EurekAlert — 200 OK; confirms 30.02% and 27.1% figures, nanoparticles as middle reflector boosting current — but does NOT specify 'silicon oxide nanoparticles'; just says 'nanoparticles.' The specific material composition is from the Nature paper. (4) Interesting Engineering — 200 OK; confirms 30.02%, 27.1% previous record, three-step deposition strategy for near-infrared absorption.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All core claims verified. Two minor attribution notes: (1) 'Silicon oxide nanoparticles... according to EurekAlert' — EurekAlert says 'nanoparticles' without specifying silicon oxide; the material is from the Nature paper. Claim is accurate but attribution should be to Nature. (2) '4-hydroxybenzylamine' chemical name — secondary sources use general language; this level of detail is from the Nature primary paper and is correctly cited there. Open-circuit voltage '1.405 V' rounds to the '1.4V' stated by secondary sources — consistent. Overall factual accuracy is high.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and appropriately measured. Does not overstate commercial readiness — clearly notes this is a lab demonstration and manufacturing at scale remains unproven.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald perovskite or solar efficiency record coverage found. Original and timely for a March 17 Nature publication.
concerns: Attribution of 'silicon oxide nanoparticles' to EurekAlert — EurekAlert says only 'nanoparticles'; the material specification comes from the Nature paper.
recommendations: Change 'silicon oxide nanoparticles... according to EurekAlert' attribution to cite the Nature paper instead — the specific material composition is in the primary source, not EurekAlert. Not blocking for this review.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approve. Accurate, well-sourced, and appropriate in scope. The silicon oxide attribution note is minor and not blocking — the claim is factually correct, just attributed to the wrong secondary source.
